subtopic_detail: Urea cycle disorder - hyperammonaemia emergency management
stem: A 3-day-old term male infant presents with progressive lethargy, poor feeding, and vomiting since yesterday. He was born via uncomplicated vaginal delivery at 39 weeks with a normal birth weight and was breastfeeding well until 36 hours ago. On examination he is tachypnoeic, hypotonic, and minimally responsive to stimulation. A generalised tonic seizure occurs during assessment.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 170, RR 55, SpO2 97% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees C
data_table.Investigations: Ammonia 480 micromol/L (ref <50), venous pH 7.52, pCO2 22 mmHg, HCO3 18 mmol/L, lactate 2.1 mmol/L, glucose 3.8 mmol/L, anion gap 12 mmol/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: IV sodium benzoate and phenylbutyrate with haemodialysis referral
options.rationale: Ammonia >400 micromol/L with respiratory alkalosis (not metabolic acidosis) and normal anion gap is characteristic of a urea cycle disorder. Nitrogen scavenger therapy reduces ammonia via alternative nitrogen excretion pathways, and haemodialysis is the most effective clearance method at this critical level. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

options.text: IV dextrose 10% bolus with insulin infusion and bicarbonate correction
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is used in organic acidaemias with ketoacidosis to promote anabolism. This infant has respiratory alkalosis with a normal anion gap, not metabolic acidosis, making organic acidaemia unlikely. (Source: ASIEM: Acute Metabolic Emergencies)

options.text: High-dose oral carnitine supplementation with dietary protein restriction
options.rationale: Carnitine supplementation is first-line for fatty acid oxidation disorders and secondary carnitine deficiency in organic acidaemias. It does not address the enzymatic defect in urea cycle disorders and will not lower ammonia at this critical level. (Source: ASIEM: Acute Metabolic Emergencies)

options.text: Emergent exchange transfusion with fresh frozen plasma replacement
options.rationale: Exchange transfusion is inefficient at removing ammonia compared to haemodialysis. It does not provide the sustained clearance required when ammonia exceeds 400 micromol/L and delays definitive treatment. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

options.text: IV biotin 10 mg loading dose with empiric thiamine and riboflavin
options.rationale: Biotin is specific treatment for biotinidase deficiency and holocarboxylase synthetase deficiency, which present with metabolic acidosis and organic aciduria rather than the respiratory alkalosis pattern seen here. (Source: RCH CPG: Hyperammonaemia)

explanation.summary: Urea cycle disorders are the most common cause of severe hyperammonaemia in neonates presenting after the first 24 hours. The hallmark is respiratory alkalosis due to direct ammonia stimulation of the brainstem respiratory centre, distinguishing them from organic acidaemias which present with metabolic acidosis. OTC deficiency is the most common UCD and is X-linked. Emergency management requires cessation of protein, high caloric intake via IV dextrose, nitrogen scavenger therapy, and haemodialysis when ammonia exceeds 400 micromol/L.
key_points: Respiratory alkalosis with severe hyperammonaemia in a neonate strongly suggests a urea cycle disorder
Nitrogen scavengers (sodium benzoate, phenylbutyrate) provide alternative pathways for nitrogen excretion bypassing the urea cycle
Haemodialysis is preferred over peritoneal dialysis or exchange transfusion for rapid ammonia clearance above 400 micromol/L
OTC deficiency is X-linked and the most common urea cycle disorder
explanation.pearls: Plasma amino acids showing elevated glutamine with low citrulline localise the defect to OTC or CPS1; urinary orotic acid is elevated in OTC deficiency but normal in CPS1 deficiency, providing definitive biochemical discrimination.
